raw_title: “Shamed,” “Embarrassed,” and “Skeptical”: How Inadvertently Sharing Fake News Influences Users’ Perceptions of the Information Environment and Social Media Use
title_en: “Shamed,” “Embarrassed,” and “Skeptical”: How Inadvertently Sharing Fake News Influences Users’ Perceptions of the Information Environment and Social Media Use
title_zh: “Shamed,” “Embarrassed,” and “Skeptical”: How Inadvertently Sharing Fake News Influences Users’ Perceptions of the Information Environment and Social Media Use
plain_summary_en: This paper studies “Shamed,” “Embarrassed,” and “Skeptical”: How Inadvertently Sharing Fake News Influences Users’ Perceptions of the Information Environment and Social Media Use. In plain terms, it asks what is happening, why it matters for public communication, and what evidence the authors use. The likely focus is Perception, Social media, Fake news, Misinformation. The abstract suggests a mixed methods approach, which makes the work useful for understanding how communication changes in everyday life.
plain_summary_zh: This paper studies “Shamed,” “Embarrassed,” and “Skeptical”: How Inadvertently Sharing Fake News Influences Users’ Perceptions of the Information Environment and Social Media Use. In plain terms, it asks what is happening, why it matters for public communication, and what evidence the authors use. The likely focus is Perception, Social media, Fake news, Misinformation. The abstract suggests a mixed methods approach, which makes the work useful for understanding how communication changes in everyday life.
research_direction_en: The paper points to an active research direction around Perception, Social media, Fake news, Misinformation, especially how institutions, platforms, and audiences shape communication outcomes.
research_direction_zh: The paper points to an active research direction around Perception, Social media, Fake news, Misinformation, especially how institutions, platforms, and audiences shape communication outcomes.
